Accordingly, the "superiority" (التفوق) would be superiority in terms of proof (الحجة) rather than sovereignty and dominion. The summary of the meaning of the sentence would be: your followers among the Christians and Muslims will have their proof prevail over the proof of those who disbelieve in you among the Jews until the Day of Resurrection. This is what the commentators have mentioned and accepted regarding the meaning of the verse.

In my view, the verse does not support this, neither in its wording nor its meaning. The apparent sense of His saying, "I will take you, and raise you toward Myself, and purify you from those who are faithless, and make those who follow you..." is an announcement of the future; that a "taking," "raising," "purification," and "making" will be realized in the future relative to the time of speaking. Furthermore, His saying, "and make those who follow you," is a good promise and glad tidings, and such things only pertain to what is to come. It is well known that the proof of the followers of Jesus (عليه السلام) is nothing but the proof of Jesus himself, which Allah mentioned within the verses of the glad tidings—the glad tidings to Mary. These proofs were superior during the presence of Jesus before the raising, and after his raising; in fact, before his raising (عليه السلام), they were more decisive in cutting off the excuses of the disbelievers and uprooting their contention, and clearer in removing their doubts. So what is the meaning of promising him (عليه السلام) that the proof of his followers will surpass the proof of his opponents in the future? Furthermore, what is the meaning of restricting this victory and superiority with His saying, "until the Day of Resurrection," when proof, in its victory, does not accept restriction by time or day? Moreover, the superiority of proof over proof remains as it is on the Day of Resurrection, as the Quran informs within the reports of the Resurrection.

**If you say:** Perhaps what is meant by the superiority of the proof is its superiority in terms of acceptability, such that people are more attentive to the proof of the followers and more obedient to it, thereby making them greater in number, firmer in pillar, and stronger in power.

**I say:** This returns either to the superiority of his true followers in terms of sovereignty and power—and the reality is contrary to that, and the possibility of it being a report of a manifestation for the followers and a superiority from them that will be realized at the end of time is not supported by the wording of the verse—or it returns to the greatness of number, meaning that his followers (عليه السلام) will surpass the disbelievers, i.e., the people of truth after Jesus will be more numerous than the people of falsehood. In addition to the fact that reality does not support this—for the people of falsehood have never ceased to grow and increase over the people of truth from the time of Jesus until our day, a gap of twenty centuries—the wording of the verse does not support it either. Superiority (الفوقية) in the verse, especially since the context is one of announcing the descent of divine displeasure upon the Jews and the encompassing of wrath upon them, only suits subjugation and dominance: either in terms of the conclusive proof (الحجة البالغة) or in terms of authority and power. As for greatness of number, it does not suit the context, as is apparent.

What ought to be said is that what was taken in the verse as a definition for the two groups is His saying, "those who follow you" and His saying, "those who are faithless." The verb only indicates realization and occurrence, rather than the state of being characterized (التلبس) which is indicated by the description (الوصف), such as "the followers" (المتبعين) and "the disbelievers" (الكافرين). The mere issuance of an act from some individuals of a nation, with the approval of the rest and the subsequent generations following the path of the predecessors and proceeding upon their way, is sufficient to attribute that act to them. Similarly, the Quran rebukes the Jews and censures them for many of the acts of their predecessors, such as killing the prophets, harming them, being arrogant toward obeying the commands of Allah and His messengers, and distorting the verses of the Book, and so on.

Accordingly, it is correct that "those who are faithless" refers to the Jews, and "those who followed" refers to the Christians, because of what issued from their leaders and predecessors in terms of faith in Jesus (عليه السلام) and following him—and it was a pleasing faith and a true following—even if Allah did not approve of their following him (عليه السلام) after the appearance of Islam, nor the following of the people of the Trinity among them before the appearance of the Islamic call.

Thus, the intent is making the Christians—who are those whose predecessors followed Jesus (عليه السلام)—above the Jews, who are those who disbelieved in Jesus (عليه السلام) and plotted against him. The purpose in this context is to explain the descent of divine displeasure upon the Jews, the occurrence of the "devising" against them, and the intensification of punishment upon their nation. This does not contradict what we mentioned—that the intent of "following" is following upon the truth—as we inferred at the beginning of the discussion, as is not hidden.

This meaning is supported by the change of style in the subsequent verse, namely His saying: "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." For if the intent of "those who followed" were only the people of truth and salvation among the Christians and Muslims, it would have been more appropriate to say: "As for those who followed you, He will pay them their rewards in full," without changing the context, as is not hidden.

There is another view: that the intent of "those who followed" is the Christians and Muslims collectively, and the verse is informing that the Jews will be under the humiliation of whoever acknowledges the necessity of following Jesus until the Day of Resurrection. The reasoning is the same, and this is the best of views in directing the verse upon reflection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ** — "Then to Me will be your return, and I will judge between you concerning that about which you used to differ" (3:55). The Glorified has gathered in this address Jesus, those who followed him, and those who disbelieved in him. This is the ultimate end of their affair on the Day of Resurrection. With this, the matter of Jesus and his report is concluded, from the time of the glad tidings of him until the end of his affair and news.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ** — "As for the faithless, I will punish them with a severe punishment in this world and the Hereafter" (3:56). Its apparent sense is that it is predicated upon His saying, "and I will judge between you," as the branching of detail from the general. Thus, it would be an explanation of the divine judgment on the Day of Resurrection regarding the punishment for the Jews who disbelieved and the full payment of reward for the believers.

However, the inclusion of "in this world" (في الدنيا) in the branching indicates that it is predicated upon the entirety of His saying: "and make those who follow you above those who are faithless... then to Me is your return," etc. Thus, it indicates that the result of this "making" and "return" is the intensification of punishment upon them in this world at the hands of those whom Allah made superior to them, and in the Hereafter by the Fire, and they shall have no helpers in that.

This is one of the evidences that the intent of "superiority" (التفوق) in the previous verse is dominance through control and power, rather than support through proof.

In His saying, "and they shall have no helpers," there is an indication of the negation of intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — that would prevent the descent of punishment upon them; it is a finalized decree, as previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ** — "But as for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them their rewards in full" (3:57). This is a good promise of fair recompense for those who followed. However, since the mere truth of "following" does not necessarily entail the entitlement to a great reward—because "following," as you have known, is a description true of the nation by its mere realization and issuance from a number of its individuals—it only produces a beautiful effect and a great reward for the one who is personally characterized by it, rather than the one who is merely attributed to it by name. Therefore, He substituted "those who followed you" with the like of His saying: "those who have faith and do righteous deeds," so that the meaning remains sound. For felicity and the good end revolve around reality rather than the name, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا وَالنَّصَارَىٰ وَالصَّابِئِينَ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve" (2:62).

This is the reward of those who have faith and do righteous deeds among those who followed Jesus (عليه السلام): that Allah will pay them their rewards in full. As for others, they have nothing of that, and this was alluded to in the verse by His saying: "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers."

From here, the secret becomes apparent in concluding the verse—which is a verse of mercy and Paradise—with the like of His saying: "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers," even though it is customary in verses of mercy and blessing to conclude with names of mercy and forgiveness, or with praise for the state of those in whose favor the verse was revealed, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْحُسْنَىٰ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ** — "To each Allah has promised the best [reward], and Allah is well aware of what you do" (57:10), and His saying: **إِن تُقْرِضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَاعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ** — "If you lend Allah a good loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you, and Allah is all-appreciative, all-forbearing" (64:17), and His saying: **وَمَن يُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَالِحًا يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّئَاتِهِ وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا أَبَدًا ذَٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ** — "Whoever has faith in Allah and acts righteously, He will absolve him of his misdeeds and admit him into gardens with conduits running through them, to remain in them forever. That is the great triumph" (64:9), and His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ** — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest triumph" (45:30), among other verses.

Thus, His saying, "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers," is intended to explain the state of the other group among those who attributed themselves to Jesus (عليه السلام) by following, but who are not those who have faith and do righteous deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ الْآيَاتِ وَالذِّكْرِ الْحَكِيمِ** — "This which We recite to you is of the signs and the Wise Remembrance" (3:58). This is an indication of the conclusion of the story. What is meant by the Wise Remembrance (الذكر الحكيم) is the Quran, which is a remembrance of Allah, firm (محكم) in its verses and explanations; falsehood does not enter it, nor does jest penetrate it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (3:59). This is a summary of the point of necessity from what He mentioned of the story of Jesus regarding his birth in detail. Conciseness after prolixity—especially in the context of argumentation and reasoning—is among the merits of speech. The verses were revealed for argumentation and addressed the state of the Christian delegation, the Christians of Najran. It was most appropriate to summarize the explanation of his creation after the detailed account of his story, to indicate that the manner of his birth does not indicate more than his being a created human, similar to Adam (عليهما السلام). Thus, it is not permissible to say regarding him more or greater than what was said regarding Adam, which is that he is a human whom Allah created without a father.

The meaning of the verse is: the likeness of Jesus with Allah—that is, his description existing with the Exalted, meaning what Allah knows of the manner of Jesus's creation which proceeded by His hand—is that the manner of his creation resembles the manner of Adam's creation. The manner of his creation was that He gathered his parts from dust, then said to him "Be," and he became a human becoming without a father.

The explanation, in reality, resolves into two arguments, each of which, in its own right, suffices to negate divinity from the Messiah (عليه السلام).

First: Jesus is a creation of Allah—according to what Allah knows, and He does not err in His knowledge—a human creation, even if he lacked a father. Whoever is such is a servant, not a lord.

Second: His creation does not exceed the creation of Adam. If the nature of his creation necessitated that his divinity be asserted in any way, the creation of Adam would have necessitated that as well, yet they do not assert it for him. Thus, it is obligatory that they do not assert it for Jesus (عليه السلام) either, due to the similarity.

It appears from the verse that the creation of Jesus, like the creation of Adam, is a natural, cosmic creation, even if it broke the established norm in lineage, which is the child's need for a father in its formation.

It is apparent that His saying, "and he was" (فيكون), was intended to narrate the past state. This does not contradict the indication of His saying, "then He said to him: Be," regarding the negation of gradualness (التدريج), for the relation is different. All these existents—whether gradual in existence or otherwise—are created by Allah, the Glorified, existing by His command, which is the word "Be" (كن), as the Exalted said: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82). Many of them are gradual in existence when their state is measured against their gradual causes. But when observed in relation to the Exalted, there is no gradualness there, nor any delay, as the Exalted said: **وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50). Further clarification of this meaning will come, if Allah wills, in its appropriate place.

Furthermore, the main point for which His saying, "then He said to him: Be," was brought is to explain that the Exalted does not need causes in creating anything, such that the state of what He wills to create would differ in relation to Him in terms of possibility and impossibility, ease and difficulty, or proximity and distance, due to the difference in the states of the causes involved in its existence. Whatever He willed and said to it "Be," it was, without need for the causes usually involved.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ** — "This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters" (3:60). This is a confirmation of the content of the previous verse after confirming it with "Indeed" (إن) and the like, similar to the confirmation of the detail of the story with His saying: "This which We recite to you is of the signs and the Wise Remembrance." In it is a comforting of the soul of the Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he is upon the truth, and an encouragement for him in the argumentation.

This—namely His saying, "The truth is from your Lord"—is among the most exquisite Quranic explanations, as He restricted the truth with "from" (من), which indicates beginning (الابتداء), rather than anything else, such as saying, "The truth is with your Lord," because of the taint of polytheism (الشرك) and the attribution of incapacity to Him, the Exalted, in reality.

That is because these true sayings and the established matters of fact (القضايا النفس الأمرية) — whatever they may be, even if they are necessary and impossible to change from what they are, such as our saying: "four is even," and "one is half of two," and the like — the human only captures them from the external reality occurring in existence, and all existence is from Him, the Exalted. Thus, all truth is from Him, just as all good is from Him. Therefore, He is not questioned about what He does, while they are questioned. For the act of another only accompanies the truth if it is true, whereas His act, the Exalted, is the existence of which the truth is but its cognitive form.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "O Mary, indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above the women of all the worlds," he said: He (عليه السلام) said: "He chose her twice: as for the first, He chose her—meaning He selected her; as for the second, she conceived without a male, so He chose her by that above the women of all the worlds."

In *al-Majma'*, Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) said: "The meaning of the verse is: He chose you for the progeny of the prophets, and purified you from fornication, and chose you for the birth of Jesus without a male."

**I say:** The meaning of his saying, "He chose you for the progeny of the prophets," is: He selected you to be a righteous descendant worthy of being attributed to the prophets. The meaning of his saying, "and purified you from fornication," is: He gave you protection (العصمة) from it, which is the main point in its context because she gave birth to Jesus without a male. Thus, the speech is intended to explain some of the implications of her being chosen and purified. The two narrations are not contradictory, as is apparent, and the indication of the verse toward that has already passed.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Hibban, and al-Hakim narrated from Anas: that the Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Sufficient for you among the women of the worlds are Mary daughter of 'Imran, Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid, Fatimah daughter of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and Asiyah the wife of Pharaoh." Al-Suyuti said: "And Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated it from al-Hasan in a detached (مرسلاً) form."

In it, al-Hakim narrated and graded authentic from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The best of the women of the worlds are Khadijah, Fatimah, Mary, and Asiyah the wife of Pharaoh."

In it, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Hasan, who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Indeed, Allah chose four above the women of the worlds: Asiyah daughter of Muzahim, Mary daughter of 'Imran, Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid, and Fatimah daughter of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Jarir narrated from Fatimah (رضي الله عنها), who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to me: "You are the lady of the women of the people of Paradise, except for Mary the Virgin (البتول)."

In it, Ibn 'Asakir narrated from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The lady of the women of the people of Paradise is Mary daughter of 'Imran, then Fatimah, then Khadijah, then Asiyah the wife of Pharaoh."

In it, Ibn 'Asakir narrated by way of Muqatil from al-Dahhak from Ibn 'Abbas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Four women are the ladies of their worlds: Mary daughter of 'Imran, Asiyah daughter of Muzahim, Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid, and Fatimah daughter of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); and the best of them in the world is Fatimah."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Layla, who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Fatimah is the lady of the women of the worlds after Mary daughter of 'Imran, Asiyah the wife of Pharaoh, and Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid."

In *al-Khisal*, with its chain to 'Ikrimah from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) drew four lines, then said: "The best of the women of Paradise are Mary daughter of 'Imran, Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid, Fatimah daughter of Muhammad, and Asiyah daughter of Muzahim, the wife of Pharaoh."

In it also, with its chain to Abu al-Hasan the First (عليه السلام), who said: The Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, chose four from among women: Mary, Asiyah, Khadijah, and Fatimah..." (the narration).

**I say:** The narrations with this approximate meaning from the paths of both groups are many. The fact that these are the ladies of women does not contradict the existence of superiority among them themselves, as appears from the sixth report quoted from *al-Durr al-Manthur* and other reports. A similar discussion has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah..." (3:33).

What should be noted is that what occurred in the verse is "choosing" (الاصطفاء); and it has already passed that it is selection. What occurred in the reports is "leadership" (السيادة); and there is a difference between them in terms of meaning, for the second is among the degrees of perfection of the first.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "when they cast their pens [to see] which of them should take charge of Mary," from al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "They cast lots with them when she was orphaned of her father."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: "And when the angels said: O Mary, indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above the women of all the worlds," he said: "He chose her twice: as for the first, He chose her—meaning He selected her; as for the second, she conceived without a male, so He chose her by that above the women of all the worlds." — until al-Qummi said — "Then He said to His Prophet: 'This is of the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, O Muhammad; and you were not with them when they cast their pens [to see] which of them should take charge of Mary, and you were not with them when they were contending.' He said: 'When she was born, the family of 'Imran contended over her, and all of them said: We shall take charge of her. So they went out and cast lots among them, and the lot of Zachariah came out...'" (the narration).

**I say:** The explanation that supports this report and the one before it has already passed.

Know that there are many narrations regarding the glad tidings of Mary, the birth of Jesus (عليه السلام), his call, and his miracles. However, what occurred in the noble verses of the summaries of his stories is sufficient for what is important in the exegetical discussion. Therefore, we have omitted mentioning them except for what is important to mention.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and I inform you of what you eat..." (the verse), from al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "Jesus used to say to the Children of Israel: 'Indeed I am the Apostle of Allah to you, and I create for you from clay the likeness of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by Allah's leave, and I heal the blind and the leper.' The blind (الأكمه) is the one born blind. They said: 'We do not see what you do except as sorcery, so show us a sign by which we may know that you are truthful.' He said: 'What if I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses'—he says: 'what you ate in your houses before you came out and what you stored at night'—'will you know that I am truthful?' They said: 'Yes.' So he would say: 'You ate such and such, and drank such and such, and put away such and such.' Some of them would accept from him and believe, and some of them would disbelieve. In that was a sign for them, if they were believers."

**I say:** The change in the context of the verse in narrating what he (عليه السلام) mentioned of the signs, first and last, supports this narration, and an indication of this has already passed.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and confirming what was before me of the Torah, and to make lawful for you..." (the verse), from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), who said: "There were four hundred years between David and Jesus. The law of Jesus was that he was sent with monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and sincerity, and with what Noah, Abraham, and Moses had been commanded. The Gospel was revealed to him, and the covenant that was taken from the prophets was taken from him. It was legislated for him in the Book: the establishment of prayer with the religion, commanding the right and forbidding the wrong, the prohibition of the forbidden and the making lawful of the lawful. There were revealed to him in the Gospel exhortations, parables, and limits (الحدود) in which there was no retaliation (القصاص), nor laws of prescribed punishments, nor the obligation of inheritance. There was revealed to him a lightening of what was upon Moses in the Torah, and that is the saying of Allah in what Jesus said to the Children of Israel: 'and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you.' Jesus commanded those with him among the believers who followed him to believe in the law of the Torah and the Gospel."

**I say:** He narrated the narration in *Qisas al-Anbiya* in detail from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and in it: "There were four hundred and eighty years between David and Jesus." Neither of them agrees with the history of the People of the Book.

In *al-'Uyun*, from al-Rida (عليه السلام): He was asked why the disciples (الحواريون) were called *al-Hawariyyun*? He said: "As for what is with the people, they were called *Hawariyyun* because they were fullers (قصارين) who purified clothes from dirt by washing, and it is a name derived from *al-khubz al-huwar* (white bread). As for what is with us, the disciples were called *Hawariyyun* because they were sincere (مخلصين) in themselves and purified from the dirt of sins through exhortation and reminder."

In *al-Tawhid*, from him (عليه السلام): "They were twelve men, and the best and most learned of them was Luke."

In *al-Ikmal*, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "Allah sent Jesus son of Mary, and entrusted him with light, knowledge, wisdom, and all the sciences of the prophets before him, and added for him the Gospel. He sent him to Jerusalem to the Children of Israel, calling them to His Book and His wisdom, and to faith in Allah and His Apostle. Most of them refused except in rebellion and disbelief. When they did not believe, he called upon his Lord and was determined, so He transformed some of them into devils to show them a sign that they might take heed, but that only increased them in rebellion and disbelief. He came to Jerusalem and remained calling them and encouraging them toward what is with Allah for thirty-three years, until the Jews sought him and claimed they had tortured him and buried him in the earth alive. Some of them claimed they killed him and crucified him. Allah would not give them authority over him; rather, it was made to appear so to them. They were not able to torture him or kill him, nor to kill him and crucify him, for if they had been able to do that, it would have been a denial of His saying: 'Rather, Allah raised him up' after He had taken him."

**I say:** His saying (عليه السلام): "so He transformed some of them into devils," means He transformed a group of the wicked among them.

His saying (عليه السلام): "so he remained calling them..." is perhaps an indication of the length of his life according to what is famous, for he (عليه السلام) spoke to them from the cradle to maturity and was a prophet from his childhood, as indicated by his saying as narrated by Allah from him: **فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قَالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا** — "Then she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet’" (19:29-30).

His saying (عليه السلام): "it would have been a denial of His saying: 'Rather, Allah raised him up after He had taken him,'" is a paraphrase of His saying, the Exalted: "Rather, Allah raised him up..." (the verse), and His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed I will take you and raise you toward Myself..." (the verse). He inferred from the precedence of "taking" (التوفي) over "raising" (الرفع) in the wording the sequence between them in existence.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from al-Baqir (عليه السلام), who said: "Jesus made a promise to his companions on the night Allah raised him to Himself. They gathered with him at evening, and they were twelve men. He brought them into a house, then he came out to them from a spring in a corner of the house, shaking the water from his head. He said: 'Indeed, Allah has revealed to me that He is raising me to Himself this hour, and purifying me from the Jews. Which of you will have my likeness cast upon him, so he is killed and crucified and be with me in my degree?' A young man among them said: 'I, O Spirit of Allah.' He said: 'Then you are he.' Then Jesus said to them: 'Indeed, among you is one who will disbelieve in me before morning twelve times.' A man among them said: 'Am I he, O Prophet of Allah?' Jesus said to him: 'Do you feel that in yourself? Then be he.' Then Jesus said to them: 'Indeed, you will split after me into three sects: two sects forging lies against Allah, in the Fire; and a sect following Simon, truthful toward Allah, in Paradise.' Then Allah raised Jesus to Himself from a corner of the house while they were looking at him."

Then he said: "The Jews came seeking Jesus that night. They took the man to whom Jesus had said: 'Indeed among you is one who will disbelieve in me before morning twelve times,' and they took the young man upon whom the likeness of Jesus was cast, so he was killed and crucified. And the one to whom Jesus said he would disbelieve before morning twelve times did disbelieve."

**I say:** Something close to this was narrated from Ibn 'Abbas, Qatadah, and others. Some said: "The one upon whom the likeness of Jesus was cast was the one who guided them to seize him and kill him," and other things were said. The Quran is silent regarding that. The full discussion will come in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ** — "they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them" (4:157).

In *al-'Uyun*, from al-Rida (عليه السلام), who said: "The matter of none of the prophets of Allah and His proofs over the people was made to appear so (شبه) except the matter of Jesus alone, because he was raised from the earth alive, and his soul was taken between the heaven and the earth, then he was raised to the heaven and his soul was returned to him. That is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'When Allah said: O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you toward Myself and purify you,' and Allah said, narrating the saying of Jesus on the Day of Resurrection: 'and I was a witness over them so long as I was among them. But when You took me, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness over all things.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), who said: "Jesus son of Mary was raised wearing a woolen garment (مدرعة) from the spinning of Mary, the weaving of Mary, and the sewing of Mary. When he reached the heaven, it was called out: 'O Jesus, cast from you the adornment of the world.'"

**I say:** The clarification of the meaning of the two narrations will come at the end of Surah al-Nisa, if Allah wills.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah..." (the verse), 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir narrated from Qatadah, who said: "It was mentioned to us that the two leaders of the people of Najran and their two bishops, the *Sayyid* and the *'Aqib*, met the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) and asked him about Jesus. He said: 'Every human has a father, so what is the matter with Jesus having no father?' So Allah sent down regarding him this verse: 'Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah...'" (the verse).

**I say:** Something close to this was narrated from al-Suddi, 'Ikrimah, and others. Al-Qummi also narrated in his *Tafsir* the revelation of the verse in this instance.

## (Another Narrative Discussion on the Meaning of the *Muhaddath*)

In *al-Basa'ir*, from Zurarah, who said: I asked Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the Apostle (الرسول), the Prophet (النبي), and the *Muhaddath* (المحدث). He said: "The Apostle is the one who beholds the angel who comes to him with the message from his Lord, saying: 'He commands you such and such.' The Apostle is a prophet along with the message."

"The Prophet does not behold the angel; the thing—the news—descends upon his heart, so he becomes like one fainting and sees in his sleep." I said: "What is his knowledge that what he saw in his sleep is true?" He said: "Allah makes it clear until he knows that it is true, and he does not behold the angel. And the *Muhaddath* is the one who hears the voice but does not see a witness (شاهداً)."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Kafi* from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام). His saying "a witness" means a speaker present. It could be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the subject of "does not see."

In it also, from Burayd from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليهما السلام) in a hadith, Burayd said: "What are the Apostle, the Prophet, and the *Muhaddath*?" He said: "The Apostle is the one to whom the angel appears and speaks to him. The Prophet sees in sleep, and sometimes prophethood and messengership are gathered for one. And the *Muhaddath* is the one who hears the voice but does not see the form." I said: "May Allah set you right, how does he know that what he saw in sleep is the truth and that it is from the angel?" He said: "He is given success for that until he recognizes it. Allah has sealed the books with your Book and the prophets with your Prophet..." (the hadith).

In it, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I mentioned the *Muhaddath* in the presence of Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام). He said: "He hears the voice but does not see the form." I said: "May Allah set you right, how does he know it is the speech of the angel?" He said: "He is given tranquility (السكينة) and dignity (الوقار) until he knows it is an angel."

In it also, from Abu Basir from him (عليه السلام), who said: "'Ali was a *Muhaddath*, and Salman was a *Muhaddath*." I said: "What is the sign of the *Muhaddath*?" He said: "The angel comes to him and whispers (ينكت) into his heart such and such."

In it, from Humran ibn A'yan, who said: Abu Ja'far (عليهما السلام) informed me that 'Ali was a *Muhaddath*. Our companions said: "You have done nothing unless you asked him: who speaks to him?" It so happened that I met Abu Ja'far and said: "Did you not inform me that 'Ali was a *Muhaddath*?" He said: "Yes." I said: "Who used to speak to him?" He said: "An angel." I said: "Should I say he is a prophet or an apostle?" He said: "No, rather say his likeness is like the companion of Solomon and the companion of Moses, and his likeness is like Dhu al-Qarnayn. Have you not heard that 'Ali was asked about Dhu al-Qarnayn, whether he was a prophet? He said: 'No, but he was a servant who loved Allah, so He loved him, and he was sincere toward Allah, so He was sincere toward him.' This is his likeness."

**I say:** The narrations regarding the meaning of the *Muhaddath* — *the one spoken to by angels without being a prophet* — from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt are very many, narrated in *al-Basa'ir*, *al-Kafi*, *al-Kanz*, *al-Ikhtisas*, and others. It is also found in the narrations of the Ahl al-Sunnah.

As for the difference mentioned in the reports between the Prophet, the Apostle, and the *Muhaddath*, the discussion on the difference between the Apostle and the Prophet has already passed. Revelation (الوحي) — *the divine communication to a servant* — in the sense of Allah speaking to His servant, necessitates certain knowledge (العلم اليقيني) by its very essence without need for a proof. Its likeness in divine impartations is like the self-evident sciences (العلوم البديهية) which do not need a justificatory cause like a syllogism or the like for their acquisition by the human.

As for sleep, the narrations, as you see, interpret it in a sense other than the customary meaning—namely the vision a human sees in ordinary sleep occurring to him in his day and night. Rather, it is a state resembling fainting in which the senses of the human prophet become still, and he witnesses at that time something similar to what we witness in wakefulness. Then Allah, the Glorified, supports him by bestowing upon his soul the certainty that it is from the side of Allah, the Glorified, not from the manipulation of Satan.

As for "speaking to" (التحديث), it is the hearing of the angel's voice, except that it is with the hearing of the heart rather than the hearing of the senses. It is not of the nature of a mental thought (الخطور الذهني) which is only called "hearing a voice" by a kind of remote metaphor. Therefore, you see that the narrations combine in it between hearing the voice and "whispering into the heart," yet they call it "speaking to" and "addressing." Thus, the *Muhaddath* hears the voice of the angel in his speaking and perceives it with his hearing, similar to what we hear of customary speech and sounds heard in the material world, except that no one else shares with him in what he hears of the angel's speech. Therefore, it is a matter of the heart.

As for his knowledge that what he was spoken to with is the speech of the angel and not from the prompting of Satan, that is by support from Allah, the Glorified, and guidance, as indicated by what is in the narration of Muhammad ibn Muslim: "He is given tranquility and dignity until he knows it is an angel." That is because the satanic prompting is either falsehood in its false form to the believing human—so it is apparent that it is not from the speech of the honored angels who do not disobey Allah—or it is falsehood in the form of truth and will be followed by falsehood. The divine light that accompanies the believing servant clarifies its state. The Exalted said: **أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people..." (6:122).

Furthermore, prompting and whispering are never free from agitation in the soul and trembling in the heart, just as the remembrance of Allah and His speech are inseparable from dignity and inner peace. The Exalted said: **ذَٰلِكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَاءَهُ** — "That is only Satan frightening his allies" (3:175), and He said: **أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ** — "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28), and He said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذَا مَسَّهُمْ طَائِفٌ مِّنَ الشَّيْطَانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ** — "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they see clearly" (7:201). Thus, tranquility and peace when something is imparted to the human of speech or thought is a proof of its being a merciful impartation, just as agitation and anxiety are proofs of its being a satanic impartation; and haste, distress, and levity are attached to that.

As for what is in the narrations that the *Muhaddath* hears the voice but does not behold the angel, it is understood as the primary aspect rather than a mutual exclusion between the two meanings. This means that the criterion for a human being a *Muhaddath* is that he hears the voice without the necessity of seeing. If it happens that he witnesses the angel while hearing the voice, it is not because he is a *Muhaddath*. This is because the verses are explicit regarding the seeing of some *Muhaddaths* of the angels during the speaking, such as His saying, the Exalted, regarding Mary: **فَأَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا قَالَتْ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمَٰنِ مِنكَ إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَامًا زَكِيًّا** — "Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied himself for her as a flawless human. She said, ‘I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be God-conscious!’ He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son’" (19:17-19). And His saying, the Exalted—regarding the wife of Abraham in the story of the glad tidings: **وَلَقَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوا سَلَامًا قَالَ سَلَامٌ... وَامْرَأَتُهُ قَائِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْنَاهَا بِإِسْحَاقَ وَمِن وَرَاءِ إِسْحَاقَ يَعْقُوبَ قَالَتْ يَا وَيْلَتَىٰ أَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا عَجُوزٌ وَهَٰذَا بَعْلِي شَيْخًا إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَشَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ قَالُوا أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ رَحْمَتُ اللَّهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ إِنَّهُ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ** — "Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news. They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he replied... And his wife was standing by, and she laughed; so We gave her the good news of Isaac, and of Jacob after Isaac. She said, ‘Oh, my! Shall I bear a child when I am an old woman, and this husband of mine is an old man? This is indeed a strange thing!’ They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? The mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household! Indeed He is all-laudable, all-glorious’" (11:69-73).

There is another view: that what is meant by the negated beholding is the beholding of the reality of the angel in itself, rather than its likeness (المثال) in which it embodies itself, for the verses do not establish more than the beholding of the likeness, as is apparent.

There is a third view possible, which some have entertained: that what is negated of beholding is legislative revelation (الوحي التشريعي), such that the angel appears to the *Muhaddath* and imparts to him a religious law. This would be a protection from Allah for the station of the legislators among His prophets and apostles; however, it is not free from remoteness.
